Climate change adds a major financing need. Resilience finance helps communities manage floods, drought and heat. Yet climate and development funds often use separate systems, forcing governments to prepare duplicate plans for connected problems.

Programmes work through institutions, not around them. Local ownership, country ownership, donor coordination, civil society and local partners should influence priorities, delivery and review. Otherwise aid fragmentation can make reporting easier for donors while making services harder to manage locally.

Knowledge transfer needs reciprocal accountability. Technical assistance, monitoring systems, a public results framework and independent impact evaluation can expose a delivery-capacity gap. Local government needs project funding to become a systems-strengthening investment, while participatory aid design lets affected people challenge donor decisions.

4 · Grants, loans and fiscal space

Aid should also help countries raise domestic revenue. Tax administration, customs and accountable budgeting create fiscal space that reduces permanent dependence on donors. This is not a reason to withdraw aid early; it is a reason to design support around long-term financing.

The political economy of aid cannot be ignored. Donors use aid for diplomacy, security and commercial influence. Tied aid may require purchases from donor-country firms, reducing competition. Untied aid usually gives recipients more choice and can improve value.

Conditionality is equally contested. Conditions may support anti-corruption reform or fiscal stability, but they can override democratic choice and weaken ownership. Policy conditionality should be limited, transparent and connected to achievable reforms.

Public accountability exists on both sides. Donor taxpayers deserve evidence that money is used well. Recipient citizens deserve transparency about agreements, debt and project selection. Joint aid accountability should not flow only upward to donors.

Aid cannot solve every development problem. Trade rules, debt, tax avoidance, conflict and domestic policy may matter more than individual projects. Policy coherence requires governments to examine whether one policy undermines what their aid budget claims to support.

The financing instrument matters. Grant financing protects budgets where debt sustainability is weak, whereas soft loans may suit productive investments with credible returns. A serious decision therefore examines debt distress, fiscal space and the aid-allocation trade-off created by every commitment.

Donors sometimes attach policy conditionality or provide conditional aid, while tied aid restricts procurement and untied aid leaves more choice. These tools should face an aid-integrity framework, joint aid accountability, conflict-sensitive precaution and tests of value for money and aid-diversion cost.

5 · Sustainable exits and development outcomes

The best development cooperation therefore combines urgency with patience. It can ramp up proven interventions, link up with to excluded groups and shore up local capacity. But it should also recognise limits, learn from failure and see through long enough for institutions to endure.

Development assistance also affects the relationship between central and local government. National strategies may look coherent on paper, but delivery depends on municipalities, clinics, schools and local organisations. Funding formulas should reflect actual population needs and operating costs rather than political visibility.

Another concern is predictability. Multi-year commitments allow governments to hire staff, purchase medicines and maintain infrastructure. Short annual cycles encourage temporary contracts and defensive planning. Predictability does not remove accountability; it makes accountability more meaningful because organisations can be judged against realistic plans.

Private investment can complement aid, especially in energy, transport and digital infrastructure. Yet commercial finance naturally prefers projects with revenue streams. It cannot replace grants for vaccination, basic education or support for displaced people. Blended finance should demonstrate financial additionality rather than subsidise investments that would have occurred anyway.

Gender and disability also affect access to aid. A programme may report national coverage while excluding people who cannot travel, read official forms or access digital systems. Results should therefore be broken down by region, income, gender, age and disability.

Finally, aid relationships should include mutual learning. Recipient institutions are not empty spaces waiting for imported expertise. Local professionals often possess knowledge that external consultants lack. Technical assistance works best when it supports peer exchange, long-term mentoring and local authority over adaptation.

The final test is what remains. Capacity building, domestic revenue, resource mobilisation, sustainable financing and a credible exit strategy should permit external support to taper off without making services collapse. That is a stronger measure of aid effectiveness than money disbursed on schedule.

Durable development outcomes, poverty reduction and credible development goals depend on the political economy of reform. Institutional reform, development effectiveness, financial additionality and verifiable development results matter only if programmes can be put into effect, seen through and responsibly wrapped up.

Continue to model essays

Idea-building model

5. Advanced C2 essay

Question: Does foreign aid reduce poverty when donors retain control over priorities and delivery?
Extended model · 1390 words · designed to build arguments, not imitate exam length

International aid is criticised from opposite directions. Some argue that it saves lives and expands opportunity; others claim that it distorts institutions and preserves dependence. Both views contain evidence because aid is not one instrument and recipients are not one type of country.

What determines the effect of aid is not simply the amount transferred but the relationship between finance, institutions and local power. Emergency relief can prevent death during conflict or disaster. Long-term support can finance health, education and infrastructure that poor governments could not otherwise provide. Dependency arises when programmes substitute permanently for domestic systems. Donor-funded units may pay higher salaries, attract skilled staff and report upward to foreign agencies. When donors pull out, services collapse. This is not proof that assistance was unnecessary; it is evidence that transition was poorly designed.

Local ownership is therefore central. Recipient governments and communities should define priorities, while donors contribute finance and expertise. Only when responsibility and capability remain locally can aid produce institutional development. Yet ownership cannot mean ignoring corruption or exclusion. Public money needs safeguards, independent monitoring and civil-society participation. Conditions may be justified where funds would otherwise support abuse, but excessive policy conditionality can override democratic choice.

Finance type matters. Grant financing is appropriate for humanitarian needs and countries in debt distress. Loans may fund productive infrastructure, but even concessional debt requires repayment. Aid that increases debt while basic services are cut can undermine its own objectives.

Measurement also shapes behaviour. Donors prefer visible outputs because they are easy to report. However, schools, clinics and institutions require staff, maintenance and trust. Aid agencies have often counted activities successfully, yet they have struggled to demonstrate durable system change.

The solution is not to abandon measurement but to broaden it. Evaluations should examine service quality, distribution, resilience and local financing. Failure should be reported because learning is impossible when every project is presented as a success. Aid also interacts with global rules. A donor may finance agriculture while maintaining trade barriers against processed exports. It may support governance while enabling tax avoidance. Policy coherence requires states to examine these contradictions.

Dependency can also be misunderstood. Poor countries depend on trade, finance, technology and climate conditions, not merely aid. Ending assistance does not create autonomy if debt payments, commodity prices and conflict still constrain policy. Domestic revenue is the strongest route toward independence. Tax systems, public administration and accountable budgets create fiscal space. Aid should support these capacities and include a credible exit strategy.

Had donors invested more consistently in national systems, some countries might now rely less on parallel projects. But abrupt withdrawal can destroy the very capacity needed for independence. Fair cooperation accepts that development takes time. It uses grants where debt is dangerous, coordinates donors, funds maintenance and shares decision-making. Not only should aid meet immediate needs, but it should also expand the ability of societies to finance and govern their own future. International aid can preserve dependency when it is fragmented, politically conditional and suddenly withdrawn. It can promote genuine development when it builds institutions, protects public goods and gradually makes external support less necessary.

There is also a distinction between dependency and interdependence. No modern state is fully self-financing or technologically self-sufficient. Rich countries rely on global markets, research networks and security alliances. The relevant question is whether aid reduces a recipient’s room for independent policy or expands it.

Aid can expand autonomy when it protects education, health and administrative capacity during crisis. It can reduce autonomy when priorities change with donor politics or when programmes are too fragmented for national planning. Predictable pooled finance is therefore often more empowering than dozens of highly branded projects. Local civil society plays a special role because it can hold both donors and governments accountable. However, short grants and restrictive overhead rules weaken these organisations. Donors who claim to support local ownership should finance institutional costs, staff safety and strategic planning rather than only visible activities.

Development effectiveness must also be separated from geopolitical loyalty. Aid should not disappear merely because a recipient government is diplomatically inconvenient, nor should poor governance be ignored because the state is strategically useful. Consistent standards are difficult but necessary for legitimacy. Predictability is as important as generosity. Multi-year commitments allow ministries and local organisations to employ staff, purchase supplies and plan maintenance, whereas short annual grants encourage temporary structures. Stable finance should still depend on reporting and review, but accountability becomes more meaningful when organisations can work towards realistic outcomes rather than survive one funding cycle at a time.

Civil society can reduce dependence by holding both donors and governments accountable. Yet local organisations are often given short contracts, restricted overheads and responsibility for the most dangerous work. Genuine localisation requires finance for staff, security, administration and strategic planning. A donor cannot credibly praise local ownership while retaining every important decision.

Domestic revenue remains the clearest route towards long-term autonomy. Support for tax administration, customs and transparent budgeting can create fiscal space for services after external finance declines. This transition should be gradual because abrupt withdrawal destroys capacity rather than encouraging independence. An exit strategy is successful when institutions continue functioning, not merely when the donor closes its office.

Aid should pursue equitable access for essential workers and underserved households. evidence-based policymaking, honest cost-benefit analysis and long-term public value matter more than a donor's preferred launch date.

Education support is an investment in human capital. lifelong learning, transferable skills, targeted support and intergenerational mobility should guide whether a scholarship or school programme is genuinely inclusive.

Poverty is experienced through daily conditions: unsafe drinking water, chronic stress, weak mental wellbeing and insecure livelihoods. secure employment and fewer structural barriers therefore belong inside development evaluation.

Assistance must respond to individual circumstances while meeting a defensible evidence threshold. legal safeguards, fewer employment barriers and transparent decisions protect public confidence in both local and donor institutions.

Digital targeting requires algorithmic transparency because households face information asymmetry. regulatory oversight, procedural fairness and freedom of expression protect people who contest an exclusion decision.

Aid registries should apply data minimisation for a legitimate purpose. independent oversight can close an accountability gap, while agencies build up public data systems instead of exporting sensitive records indefinitely.

Donor-funded automation should support worker augmentation, not silent job displacement. People in entry-level roles need employers to provide paid training and share productivity gains as systems modernise.

Development learning depends on funding continuity and scientific independence. mission-driven research, replication studies and open knowledge spillovers help governments distinguish a portable lesson from a one-off success.

Earth observation and satellite data can identify damaged roads and crops. climate monitoring, weather forecasting and coordinated disaster response then help direct scarce relief where the evidence shows the greatest need.

Climate aid should connect climate adaptation with adaptation finance, flood resilience and early-warning systems. Even managed retreat requires finance that protects agency and livelihoods rather than merely moving risk elsewhere.

Rural poverty deepens when biodiversity loss weakens ecosystem services. Support for soil biodiversity, nature-positive development and the reversal of pollinator decline can protect income without creating permanent grant dependence.

Aid for food security must look beyond short deliveries. Lower market concentration, more resilient supply chains, less food waste and careful management of water scarcity can make hunger prevention durable.

Urban poverty combines housing insecurity with a difficult land-use trade-off. Strong municipal delivery capacity supports sustainable urban development instead of letting short projects add to fragmented infrastructure.

A circular economy can create repair livelihoods while lowering the material footprint. Better resource productivity also reduces economic externalities and narrows the water-security gap affecting low-income settlements.

Development finance interacts with global value-chains, trade diversification and services trade. A shared trade benefit requires donor policy to acknowledge the adjustment burden carried by workers and small producers.

Projects need community consent and careful attention to resident sentiment. Avoiding local displacement, using place-based policy and pursuing resident-centred growth prevent aid-funded infrastructure from improving statistics while harming neighbours.

Finally, civic participation and institutional coordination should include displaced people and receiving communities. integration outcome indicators and a dignity-centred approach reveal whether humanitarian support expands voice as well as immediate safety.

The deepest measure of success is whether citizens gain reliable services and greater influence over public decisions. A project that survives only through external management may deliver outputs, but it has not yet produced development in the institutional sense.

Exam-length model

6. Realistic IELTS essay · approximately 300 words

Question: Some people believe rich countries should increase foreign aid, while others argue that aid creates dependency. Discuss both views and give your opinion.
Model answer · 327 words

Wealthy countries are often asked to provide more aid, especially during conflict, climate disasters and debt crises. Others argue that long-term dependence weakens national responsibility. In my view, aid should increase where needs are severe, but programmes must strengthen domestic systems and financing.

The case for more aid is strong. Grants can protect primary healthcare, education and hunger prevention when governments lack fiscal space. Humanitarian agencies may come forward rapidly during emergencies. What wealthy countries can provide is finance at a scale unavailable to the poorest states. However, permanent external funding can create fragility. Parallel projects may attract staff away from ministries, while governments postpone tax reform. When donors pull out, services may collapse. Only when aid builds local capacity can it reduce future dependence.

The best approach combines generosity with ownership. Donors should coordinate, use national systems where safeguards permit and turn over management gradually. Grants are preferable where debt is already unsustainable, while loans should finance projects with credible returns. Aid budgets have fallen in recent years, yet humanitarian and development needs have remained high. Sudden cuts therefore risk reversing progress.

Recipient governments also have responsibilities. They should improve budgeting, combat corruption and raise domestic revenue fairly. Civil society should monitor both governments and donors. Had programmes funded maintenance and local staffing from the beginning, more projects might have survived donor withdrawal. In addition, donor policy should avoid sudden reversals. Multi-year commitments allow recipient institutions to hire staff and maintain services, whereas abrupt cuts create waste even in programmes that were performing well. Domestic revenue reform is equally important. Aid should help governments improve tax administration and budgeting so external support becomes less central over time. This gradual transition is more realistic than demanding immediate self-sufficiency from countries facing debt, conflict or climate shocks.

In conclusion, wealthy countries should provide more aid where it saves lives and protects development. The aim, however, should be stronger institutions and sustainable domestic finance rather than indefinite external control.

Why the exam-length essay is strong

Direct position

The introduction distinguishes life-saving assistance from finance that can reproduce unequal control.

Causal explanation

The essay connects funding instruments, institutions and local power to durable poverty reduction.

Developed contrast

Emergency necessity is weighed against the risks of parallel delivery and donor dependence.

Policy mechanism

Local ownership, sustainable financing, open budgets and credible exits turn principle into delivery.

Recycled language

Earlier collocations return as part of the reasoning rather than as decoration.

Controlled complexity

Advanced grammar remains clear enough for realistic exam conditions.

7. Advanced grammar transformations

1. If donors coordinated better, governments would face less paperwork. (Conditional inversion)

Governments would face less paperwork were donors to coordinate better.

2. The programme ended after local financing had disappeared. (Past perfect)

The programme ended after local financing had disappeared.

3. Local ownership matters most for sustainability. (Cleft sentence)

What matters most for sustainability is local ownership.

4. Aid works only when commitments are maintained. (Negative inversion)

Only when commitments are maintained does aid work.

5. Aid saves lives and strengthens institutions. (Not only...but also)

Not only does aid save lives, but it also strengthens institutions.

6. The project was designed for impact, but it ignored maintenance. (Participle clause)

Designed for impact, the project ignored maintenance.

7. Although loans are cheap, they still increase debt. (Fronted concession)

Cheap though the loans may be, they still increase debt.

8. Donors should coordinate, evaluate and transfer ownership. (Parallelism)

Donors should coordinate, evaluate and transfer ownership.

9. Aid budgets have fallen, but needs remain high. (Present-perfect contrast)

Aid budgets have fallen, yet needs remain high.

10. The clinic closed after the donor had withdrawn. (Past perfect)

The clinic closed after the donor had withdrawn.

11. The programme lacks an exit strategy, so services collapse. (Nominalisation)

The programme’s lack of an exit strategy causes services to collapse.

12. If grants were available, debt pressure would fall. (Conditional inversion)

Debt pressure would fall were grants available.

13. Citizens distrusted the project because spending was opaque. (Cleft cause)

It was opaque spending that caused citizens to distrust the project.

14. Aid should meet needs and strengthen capacity. (Balanced recommendation)

Aid should both meet needs and strengthen capacity.

15. The agency introduced monitoring gradually, so partners could adapt. (Participle clause)

Introducing monitoring gradually, the agency gave partners time to adapt.

16. The donor changed the design after communities objected. (Emphatic do)

The donor did change the design after communities objected.

17. No factor matters more than sustainable financing. (Negative inversion)

Rarely does any factor matter more than sustainable financing.

18. The programme should be effective, accountable and durable. (Parallelism)

The programme should improve effectiveness, preserve accountability and ensure durability.

8. Native Academic Toolbox

1. Upgrade: “Aid money is wasted.” using aid effectiveness.

Weak aid effectiveness often reflects poor design, fragmentation or limited ownership.

2. Upgrade: “Countries borrow too much.” using debt distress.

Debt distress can force governments to cut essential public services.

3. Upgrade: “Donors run many small projects.” using aid fragmentation.

Aid fragmentation increases reporting costs and weakens coordination.

4. Upgrade: “Local people should decide.” using local ownership.

Local ownership improves relevance, legitimacy and sustainability.

5. Upgrade: “The project needs a plan to end.” using exit strategy.

A credible exit strategy should include finance, staffing and maintenance.

6. Upgrade: “Aid should be measured properly.” using impact evaluation.

Impact evaluation can distinguish real effects from simple activity counts.

7. Upgrade: “The country needs cheaper finance.” using concessional finance.

Concessional finance can support investment without full market borrowing costs.

8. Upgrade: “Aid should support government systems.” using budget support.

Budget support can strengthen national systems where safeguards are credible.

9. Upgrade: “Donors should work together.” using donor coordination.

Donor coordination reduces duplication and administrative burden.

10. Upgrade: “The programme needs local money.” using domestic revenue.

Domestic revenue is essential for long-term service continuity.

11. Upgrade: “Aid should reach poor groups.” using equitable access.

Equitable access should be measured across regions and social groups.

12. Upgrade: “The project must continue.” using sustainable financing.

Sustainable financing protects services after donor support declines.

13. Upgrade: “Aid should respond quickly.” using crisis response.

Crisis response should connect emergency relief with recovery planning.

14. Upgrade: “The programme needs trustworthy data.” using monitoring systems.

Monitoring systems should provide timely and comparable evidence.

15. Upgrade: “Aid policy should match trade policy.” using policy coherence.

Policy coherence prevents one government policy from undermining another.

9. IELTS Speaking

Part 1 · 15 questions

PART 1 · 1

Do you think foreign aid is important?

Suggested phrasal verbs
ramp uptaper off
Model answer · 83 words

Yes. Foreign aid is essential when a disaster overwhelms local resources or when extreme poverty prevents a government from financing basic services. Agencies may need to ramp up emergency relief quickly, but good assistance also strengthens the institutions that will remain. I would judge it by lives protected, local control and whether support can taper off without closing clinics or schools. Aid is not automatically effective, yet rejecting it entirely would ignore emergencies and severe financing gaps that domestic budgets cannot meet alone.

PART 1 · 2

Would you donate to an aid organisation?

Suggested phrasal verbs
link up withsee through
Model answer · 83 words

I would, but I would first examine the organisation's accounts, safeguarding policy and relationship with local partners. I prefer charities that link up with established community groups instead of arriving with a predesigned solution. I would also look for evidence that they see through multi-year commitments and publish failures as well as successes. A compelling photograph might prompt my first donation, but transparent spending and respectful local ownership would determine whether I continued. Trust should come from verifiable practice, not emotional advertising alone.

PART 1 · 3

Should aid focus on health or education?

Suggested phrasal verbs
shore upput into effect
Model answer · 86 words

I would avoid a single choice because health and education reinforce each other. A child cannot learn when illness or hunger keeps them absent, while educated adults are better able to use health information and claim services. In an epidemic, aid may need to shore up primary healthcare first. In a stable area with low enrolment, school access may be the priority. Evidence should decide which plan to put into effect, and budgets should preserve the connection between both systems rather than create two competing projects.

PART 1 · 4

Do you trust international charities?

Suggested phrasal verbs
strip awaycome forward
Model answer · 86 words

I trust some of them conditionally, not automatically. A familiar logo does not prove that money reaches the people shown in an appeal. I check independent audits, overhead explanations, safeguarding records and whether communities can complain safely. Good reporting should strip away impressive totals and show who actually benefited. I also value organisations that come forward when a project fails and explain what changed. International reach can be useful during crises, but credibility depends on local partnership, transparent evidence and a willingness to be held accountable.

PART 1 · 5

Should rich countries give more aid?

Suggested phrasal verbs
ramp updial back
Model answer · 85 words

In many cases, yes, especially where climate shocks, conflict or debt distress have sharply reduced fiscal space. However, the amount is only one question. Rich countries should ramp up predictable grants for essential services while ending tied contracts that send much of the budget back to donor companies. They should also dial back harmful trade, tax or debt practices that undermine the same development goals. More finance is justified when it is additional, transparent and locally directed, rather than a substitute for fairer international rules.

PART 1 · 6

Is emergency relief more important than long-term aid?

Suggested phrasal verbs
come forwardturn over
Model answer · 85 words

Emergency relief is more urgent because delay can cost lives, but it is not inherently more important. When floods destroy homes, agencies must come forward with clean water, shelter and cash immediately. At the same time, they should work through capable public and community systems where possible. Recovery plans need to turn over responsibility, records and assets in an orderly way. Treating every crisis as a short event can leave the same population exposed again, so immediate protection and long-term resilience should be designed together.

PART 1 · 7

Do you read about development issues?

Suggested phrasal verbs
strip awaylink up with
Model answer · 84 words

Yes, although I try to use more than campaign summaries. I read reporting from local journalists, research organisations and public agencies because each source reveals different incentives. Good analysis should strip away national averages and explain who gained, who was excluded and how results were measured. I also try to link up with first-person accounts through reputable community publications. Development debates can become abstract very quickly, so combining data with local experience helps me evaluate policy without reducing people to statistics or inspirational stories.

PART 1 · 8

Should aid money be tracked publicly?

Suggested phrasal verbs
bring onlineput into effect
Model answer · 79 words

Yes, unless publication would expose vulnerable recipients or sensitive security details. Governments and donors should bring online accessible budgets that show commitments, actual disbursements, contractors and results. The information must be understandable to residents, not only auditors. Public tracking also needs complaint procedures and institutions able to put into effect corrective action when irregularities appear. Transparency alone cannot prevent corruption, but it makes hidden changes harder and gives journalists, parliaments and communities evidence with which to ask precise questions.

PART 1 · 9

Would you work for an aid organisation?

Suggested phrasal verbs
see throughturn over
Model answer · 86 words

I would consider it if the role used my skills and the organisation shared decision-making with local staff. I would want a contract, realistic security support and enough time to see through commitments rather than move between short projects. I would also ask whether leadership and budgets are eventually turned over to national or community institutions. The sector can offer meaningful work, but it can also reproduce unequal salaries and authority. I would prefer a position that transfers expertise in both directions and leaves durable capacity.

PART 1 · 10

Can small donations make a difference?

Suggested phrasal verbs
link up withshore up
Model answer · 86 words

Yes, especially when many donors support a well-run local programme. A small monthly contribution can help an organisation plan staff and supplies more reliably than one dramatic gift. Digital platforms may also link up with community funds that understand a neighbourhood's priorities. However, donors should not exaggerate what one payment achieves. Small gifts can shore up scholarships, clinics or emergency cash, but durable poverty reduction also requires public institutions, fair taxation and economic opportunity. Their value is real when it forms part of a credible system.

PART 1 · 11

Should local groups manage more aid?

Suggested phrasal verbs
turn overshore up
Model answer · 85 words

Generally, yes, because local organisations understand language, institutions and risks that an external team may miss. Donors should turn over more budget authority rather than use local groups only to deliver decisions made elsewhere. They may first need to shore up financial systems, safeguarding and staff protection, but capacity requirements should be proportionate. Local management is not automatically honest or inclusive, so community oversight remains essential. The aim is meaningful authority with shared accountability, not a romantic assumption that every local institution represents everyone equally.

PART 1 · 12

Do you think aid is sometimes wasted?

Suggested phrasal verbs
pare downstrip away
Model answer · 86 words

Yes. Money can be wasted through duplicated reporting, unsuitable equipment, foreign consultants or projects designed to fit donor announcements rather than local needs. Evaluation should strip away activity counts and ask whether services improved after funding ended. Donors may need to pare down poorly performing programmes, but abrupt cuts can waste earlier investment and harm recipients. The better response is transparent review, corrective support and, when necessary, an orderly closure. Admitting waste is part of accountability; it is not proof that every form of aid fails.

PART 1 · 13

Should donor countries attach conditions?

Suggested phrasal verbs
ease indial back
Model answer · 85 words

Only limited conditions that protect the purpose of the funds, such as open procurement, independent audit and basic safeguards. Donors should ease in new reporting requirements when local systems need time to adapt, rather than impose a complex template immediately. They should dial back conditions that force unrelated economic or political choices. Some oversight is necessary because aid uses public money, but conditions become illegitimate when a donor controls domestic policy without bearing the consequences. The standard should be proportionality, transparency and realistic local capacity.

PART 1 · 14

Is climate finance part of aid?

Suggested phrasal verbs
ramp upshore up
Model answer · 86 words

It can overlap with aid, but it should not simply replace existing development budgets. Low-income countries often contributed little to climate change yet face high adaptation costs, so richer states should ramp up genuinely additional climate finance. Grants can shore up flood protection, drought planning and resilient health systems without increasing debt distress. Clear accounting matters because relabelling an ordinary aid project as climate finance creates the appearance of two commitments from one payment. The categories may intersect, but the obligations and results should remain transparent.

PART 1 · 15

Should aid continue during political conflict?

Suggested phrasal verbs
come forwardtaper off
Model answer · 86 words

Humanitarian assistance should usually continue because civilians should not lose food or medicine for decisions made by political leaders. Neutral agencies may need to come forward through trusted local networks while monitoring diversion and staff safety. Direct government support might be suspended when authorities misuse it, but that is different from abandoning the population. Any programme that must taper off should protect essential services and communicate the change clearly. Conflict makes aid harder to deliver; it does not remove the moral importance of meeting basic needs.

Part 3 · 15 questions

PART 3 · 1

Does aid create dependency?

Suggested phrasal verbs
taper offshore up
Model answer · 123 words

Aid can create dependency when it finances permanent services through temporary parallel structures, pays salaries that public institutions cannot sustain or gives donors continuing control over priorities. Yet dependency is not an automatic property of external finance. A grant that shores up vaccination, tax administration or teacher training can expand domestic capability. Design and political incentives are decisive. Programmes should establish local budget commitments, transfer authority and set conditions under which support can taper off safely. Abrupt withdrawal is not proof of independence; it may simply destroy useful capacity. The relevant question is whether assistance increases the ability to decide, finance and deliver without the donor.

Dependency should therefore be tested through lasting capacity and choice, not the mere presence of external funding.

PART 3 · 2

Should aid be given as grants or loans?

Suggested phrasal verbs
ease insee through
Model answer · 122 words

The choice should depend on the purpose of the finance, the expected return and the recipient's debt position. Grants are preferable for emergency relief, basic health and programmes whose benefits are broad but do not generate direct revenue. Concessional loans may be reasonable for infrastructure that raises productivity and produces a credible repayment stream. Even then, lenders should ease in repayments after construction and publish debt assumptions. They must also see through maintenance and capacity commitments, not merely finance the opening ceremony. Where debt distress is already high, adding loans can shrink fiscal space and undermine the development objective the project was intended to serve.

Independent review should compare promised returns with actual maintenance costs throughout the full life of the asset.

PART 3 · 3

How can donors prevent corruption?

Suggested phrasal verbs
strip awaybring online
Model answer · 118 words

No single audit can prevent corruption. Donors need transparent procurement, independent verification, protected whistleblowers and meaningful community monitoring. They should bring online contract and disbursement data in formats that journalists and residents can use, while protecting personal information. Analysis must strip away national totals to identify inflated prices, politically connected contractors or districts that repeatedly receive less. However, creating a parallel foreign control unit can weaken public institutions and shift corruption elsewhere. The stronger approach builds capable national audit bodies, shares risk intelligently and applies proportionate sanctions to organisations and individuals, rather than punishing an entire population by cancelling essential services.

Regular publication of investigations and recovered funds would show whether controls produce consequences rather than paperwork alone.

PART 3 · 4

Is budget support better than project aid?

Suggested phrasal verbs
put into effectdial back
Model answer · 120 words

Budget support can strengthen national planning because finance enters the public system and can cover recurring costs such as teachers or medicines. It also reduces fragmented donor projects. Yet it requires credible public financial management and political accountability. Project aid may be safer in a severe governance crisis or useful for testing a focused intervention, but separate units often disappear later. A mixed approach is therefore sensible: support the budget where institutions can put into effect agreed plans, while using transparent projects for specific risks. As capacity improves, donors can dial back parallel delivery. The best instrument is the one that improves services and leaves local systems more coherent.

The comparison should be repeated as governance and sector conditions change.

PART 3 · 5

Should aid be conditional?

Suggested phrasal verbs
ease inpare down
Model answer · 116 words

Some conditions are legitimate because donors and recipient governments owe citizens evidence that money is protected and used for its stated purpose. Requirements for audit, safeguarding and publication can reinforce accountability. Problems arise when donors attach broad ideological reforms unrelated to the programme or demand changes too quickly. Conditions should be jointly agreed, measurable and sensitive to administrative capacity. New controls can be eased in with technical support, while duplicated indicators should be pared down. Suspension should be a last resort with protections for essential services. Conditionality is defensible when it safeguards results; it becomes coercive when financial leverage replaces political consent.

Affected communities should also help define breaches and remedies before any agreement is signed.

PART 3 · 6

How can aid work in fragile states?

Suggested phrasal verbs
link up withshore up
Model answer · 118 words

Aid in fragile states must manage immediate need without legitimising abuse or bypassing institutions forever. Donors can link up with municipalities, professional bodies, community organisations and carefully vetted ministries, using different channels for different risks. Flexible multi-year grants can shore up health, water and social protection while security conditions change. Programmes also need conflict-sensitive analysis so that beneficiary selection does not deepen local tensions. Remote monitoring may help, but it cannot replace trusted local knowledge. Success should be defined realistically: protecting essential functions, preserving staff and expanding accountable capacity may matter more than ambitious institutional reform during an active crisis.

Longer funding horizons also help capable local teams retain staff instead of repeatedly rebuilding them after short grants.

PART 3 · 7

Why is local ownership important?

Suggested phrasal verbs
turn oversee through
Model answer · 120 words

Local ownership matters because residents and public institutions understand priorities, trade-offs and implementation constraints that external experts may overlook. It also makes accountability more meaningful: people can question decision-makers who remain after the donor leaves. Ownership must involve budget authority and agenda setting, not only consultation after a project is designed. Donors should turn over decisions as capacity grows and finance local organisations' indirect costs, staff development and safeguarding. At the same time, local elites are not automatically representative, so women, minorities and service users need voice. Programmes are more likely to be seen through when those responsible consider them their own commitments.

Ownership is strongest when communities can alter, reject and evaluate a proposal, not simply endorse it publicly.

PART 3 · 8

Can private investment replace aid?

Suggested phrasal verbs
ramp uppull out
Model answer · 123 words

Private investment can create jobs, infrastructure and tax revenue at a scale aid budgets rarely match, but it cannot replace every function of aid. Investors reasonably seek returns, so they may avoid remote regions, fragile states or services for people with little purchasing power. They can also pull out when risk rises, precisely when social protection is most necessary. Public and concessional finance can ramp up guarantees or early infrastructure where this creates genuine additional investment, but contracts must protect the public interest. Aid remains necessary for humanitarian relief, institutional capacity and essential services whose development dividend is large even when commercial revenue is small.

Strong labour, environmental and tax rules are necessary so private gains do not transfer new costs to communities.

PART 3 · 9

Should donors prioritise climate finance?

Suggested phrasal verbs
ramp upshore up
Model answer · 115 words

Climate finance deserves a much higher priority because droughts, floods and heat can erase gains in health, education and livelihoods. Donors should ramp up grants for adaptation in countries with low historic emissions and limited fiscal space. The money can shore up water systems, early warnings, resilient crops and public health. However, climate funding should not displace commitments to vaccination, schooling or poverty reduction, since these systems also determine resilience. Priorities should be locally defined and additional finance clearly counted. A climate label is not enough; programmes need evidence that they reduce exposure, protect vulnerable groups and remain maintainable under future conditions.

Loss-and-damage support is also necessary where adaptation can no longer prevent severe harm.

PART 3 · 10

How should aid effectiveness be measured?

Suggested phrasal verbs
strip awaysee through
Model answer · 115 words

Aid effectiveness should be measured through outcomes, distribution, cost and durability rather than money spent or activities completed. Evaluators must strip away averages and ask whether women, rural households, minorities and people with disabilities gained equal access. Baselines and comparison methods should be credible, but quantitative results need local explanation. A programme that raises school attendance for one year may still fail if teachers and budgets disappear later. Donors should therefore see through post-project evaluation and publish negative findings. Effectiveness also includes institutional change: stronger procurement, local authority and sustainable financing may be valuable even when their benefits take longer to become visible.

Recipient institutions should help choose the indicators and own the underlying data.

PART 3 · 11

What happens when donors cut budgets suddenly?

Suggested phrasal verbs
pare downwrap up
Model answer · 117 words

Sudden cuts can interrupt medicines, salaries, school meals and cash transfers before governments or local organisations can replace the finance. Skilled staff leave, suppliers lose confidence and earlier training becomes wasted. Communities may also conclude that consultation was meaningless. Donors sometimes must pare down budgets because of fiscal or political change, but they should give notice, protect life-saving services and negotiate transition finance. Programmes need to wrap up in an orderly sequence, with records, assets and responsibilities transferred. A credible exit strategy cannot remove every loss, yet it can prevent a budget decision in one capital from becoming an unmanaged service crisis elsewhere.

Transparent notice also allows other funders to coordinate rather than duplicate an emergency response.

PART 3 · 12

Should countries receive aid if elites are corrupt?

Suggested phrasal verbs
come forwardlink up with
Model answer · 118 words

Yes, people should not be denied vaccines, water or emergency food because elites are corrupt. However, donors should change the channel and strengthen safeguards. They can link up with audited local governments, civil-society organisations, multilateral agencies or direct cash systems, depending on the sector and risk. Independent monitors and citizens must be able to come forward safely with evidence of diversion. Completely withdrawing aid can strengthen corrupt elites by weakening alternative institutions and increasing desperation. At the same time, unconditional budget transfers may be indefensible. The ethical task is to protect the population while limiting capture, publishing trade-offs and supporting institutions capable of future accountability.

This balance demands more careful management than either unrestricted finance or moral withdrawal.

PART 3 · 13

Can aid reduce migration pressure?

Suggested phrasal verbs
shore uppull out
Model answer · 118 words

Aid may reduce forced movement when it shores up safety, livelihoods and public services, but it is not a reliable tool for stopping migration in general. Development can initially increase mobility because people gain education, information and resources to move. Conflict, persecution and family ties also cannot be solved by a small local project. Donors should not threaten to pull out unless governments prevent departures, since that can distort priorities and undermine rights. The legitimate aim is to expand meaningful choices: people should not have to leave to survive, but neither should assistance be designed to confine them where opportunity or safety is absent.

Outcome reporting should therefore measure wellbeing and agency, not merely a fall in departures.

PART 3 · 14

How can debt undermine development?

Suggested phrasal verbs
dial backpare down
Model answer · 116 words

High debt service reduces fiscal space, forcing governments to pare down health, education or infrastructure even when needs are rising. Currency depreciation can make foreign-denominated payments more expensive, while short maturities create repeated refinancing risk. Creditors may then demand rapid austerity that weakens growth and public confidence. Debt is not inherently harmful: concessional borrowing for productive investment can create returns above its cost. The danger comes from opaque contracts, unrealistic forecasts and loans for projects without durable value. Responsible lenders should dial back new exposure where debt sustainability is weak, disclose terms and support restructuring before arrears become a full social crisis.

Parliaments and citizens need the same information before borrowing, not after repayment problems emerge.

PART 3 · 15

What would fair development cooperation look like?

Suggested phrasal verbs
turn oversee through
Model answer · 121 words

Fair cooperation would begin with priorities defined by affected communities and accountable public institutions, not a donor's procurement calendar. Finance would be predictable, mostly grant-based where debt risk is high, and untied so local suppliers can compete. Donors would turn over authority as capacity grows while remaining answerable for their own conditions, delays and policy contradictions. Partners would agree a small set of verifiable results, publish budgets and protect safe complaints. They would also see through long-term commitments in health, education, revenue and climate resilience. The goal would not be permanent generosity, but stronger local choice, sustainable financing and relationships in which knowledge and scrutiny move in both directions.

Both sides would be able to challenge assumptions without risking the relationship.

10. Five IELTS Writing Task 2 topics

Before writing: check that each body paragraph has a clear topic sentence, explanation, development and a relevant consequence or example. Your position must remain consistent from the introduction to the conclusion.
TASK 2 · 1

Foreign aid should be provided mainly as grants rather than loans. To what extent do you agree or disagree?

Optional collocation bank
debt sustainabilitydevelopment dividendfiscal spacedebt distressease inofficial aiddevelopment assistancehumanitarian aidbilateral aid
Model essay · 308 words

Foreign aid can be delivered through grants or loans, and the right choice depends on both purpose and repayment capacity. I largely agree that grants should be the main instrument, particularly for basic services and crisis recovery, although carefully designed concessional loans can finance productive infrastructure when debt sustainability is secure.

Grants are most appropriate where a project creates a broad development dividend but little direct revenue. Vaccination, teacher training and clean-water systems improve wellbeing and productivity, yet a ministry cannot charge every beneficiary enough to repay a loan. Grant financing also protects fiscal space in countries already facing debt distress, allowing essential spending to continue rather than forcing future governments to choose between creditors and citizens.

Loans nevertheless have a legitimate role. A reliable electricity grid, port or irrigation system may raise output and public revenue over decades. If forecasts are conservative, terms transparent and interest genuinely concessional, repayment can spread the cost across the generations that receive the benefit. Loans may also encourage rigorous appraisal, although this discipline disappears when lenders expect geopolitical influence or assume that another creditor will absorb failure.

The decisive safeguards are independent debt analysis, open procurement and local authority over priorities. Donors should offer grants where returns are mainly social and reserve soft loans for investments with credible cash flows or tax gains. Repayments should be eased in after benefits begin, and lenders must publish stress tests. No government should be encouraged to borrow merely because a donor wants to announce a large commitment.

In conclusion, grants should form the core of aid because poverty reduction and essential services should not deepen sovereign debt. Concessional loans remain useful for selected productive assets, but only when benefits, risks and repayment capacity are demonstrated publicly. Matching the instrument to the purpose is more responsible than treating every transfer as either charity or credit.

TASK 2 · 2

Some people believe aid should be conditional on political reform, while others think conditions undermine national sovereignty. Discuss both views and give your opinion.

Optional collocation bank
recipient accountabilityvalue for moneyput into effectparticipatory aid designofficial aiddevelopment assistancehumanitarian aidbilateral aidmultilateral aid
Model essay · 308 words

Whether donors should require political reform in exchange for aid is a difficult question about accountability and power. Conditions can protect public money and human rights, but they can also let wealthy governments dictate policies whose consequences they do not bear. In my view, narrowly targeted safeguards are justified, whereas broad political conditionality usually undermines legitimate national choice.

Supporters argue that unconditional finance may strengthen corrupt or repressive elites. Requirements for transparent procurement, independent audit and non-discrimination can prevent diversion and ensure that citizens receive the intended service. A donor also has obligations to its taxpayers. If evidence shows that a ministry is stealing health funds, continuing identical budget support would contradict both recipient accountability and value for money.

Critics respond that political conditions are rarely neutral. Donors may demand privatisation, electoral rules or foreign-policy alignment that has little connection to the funded programme. Because aid-dependent governments have limited bargaining power, formal consent can conceal coercion. Conditions may also punish ordinary people when finance for medicine or schools is suspended, while politically connected elites remain protected. This weakens country ownership and can reduce reform to compliance with an external checklist.

A better approach distinguishes programme integrity from political control. Partners should jointly agree a small number of measurable safeguards linked directly to the funds, publish them before disbursement and provide support to put into effect new systems. Serious abuse may require switching delivery channels, but life-saving services should be protected. Wider constitutional or economic reform should emerge through domestic institutions and participatory aid design, not an ultimatum from a creditor.

In conclusion, aid cannot be free of conditions because financial and safeguarding rules are necessary. However, donors should not use assistance as leverage for an entire political agenda. Proportionate, transparent and programme-specific requirements can protect results while respecting sovereignty far better than sweeping conditions attached to urgent needs.

TASK 2 · 3

International organisations increasingly provide direct cash transfers during crises. Do the advantages outweigh the disadvantages?

Optional collocation bank
shore upramp updata minimisationofficial aiddevelopment assistancehumanitarian aidbilateral aidmultilateral aidaid effectiveness
Model essay · 300 words

Direct cash transfers give crisis-affected households money rather than predetermined goods. Although cash can be diverted or lose value when markets fail, I believe its advantages generally outweigh the disadvantages where shops function, recipients can access payments safely and strong monitoring is in place.

The principal advantage is choice. A family may need rent, medicine or transport rather than the standard package selected by an external agency. Cash protects dignity and can shore up local markets because households purchase from nearby traders. Digital delivery may also be faster and cheaper than transporting large quantities of food, especially when roads are damaged but payment networks still operate.

There are genuine risks. Inflation can erode the transfer, armed groups may demand a share and digital registration can exclude people without documents or phones. Publishing beneficiary names may expose them to theft, while weak market supply can simply raise prices. Cash also cannot replace public goods such as clean water, vaccination or shelter infrastructure, which require coordinated provision.

Agencies should therefore conduct market and protection assessments before they ramp up payments. Recipients need several safe ways to register, collect funds and complain. Transfer values should adjust to prices, while data minimisation limits exposure of personal information. Independent monitoring can compare cash with in-kind aid, and programmes should switch methods if supply collapses or coercion increases. Local organisations must help define these thresholds.

In conclusion, direct cash is not universally suitable, but it usually gives households more relevant and timely support than a fixed package. Its advantages outweigh the risks when agencies protect access, monitor markets and retain other forms of assistance for collective needs. The strongest crisis response treats cash as a flexible instrument, not a complete substitute for functioning services. Continuous feedback should determine when its value or delivery method must change.

TASK 2 · 4

Many development projects end when donor funding stops. What problems does this cause, and how can programmes become more sustainable?

Optional collocation bank
aid-withdrawal spilloverscapacity buildingsustainable financingtaper offturn overofficial aiddevelopment assistancehumanitarian aidbilateral aid
Model essay · 300 words

When a project collapses as soon as donor funding ends, communities lose services and the earlier investment produces little durable value. This problem usually reflects parallel delivery, unfunded recurring costs and weak transfer of authority. Sustainability requires programmes to build local systems and secure finance from the beginning rather than add an exit plan during the final month.

Abrupt closure can interrupt medicines, school meals, agricultural advice or staff salaries. Trained workers leave, equipment deteriorates without maintenance and residents become less willing to trust the next consultation. Governments may inherit an expensive model they never selected and cannot afford. These aid-withdrawal spillovers can even weaken existing institutions if the project previously attracted their best employees with temporary higher pay.

Sustainability begins with realistic design. Local ministries, councils and community organisations should decide priorities, staffing levels and technology. Donor finance should use national systems where safeguards permit, while capacity building focuses on functions that staff will actually perform. Budgets must include maintenance, replacement and supervision, not merely construction and training targets.

Programmes also need a staged financing transition. Domestic revenue, service contracts or pooled donor funds may provide sustainable financing, depending on the sector and people's ability to pay. External support should taper off only after agreed readiness indicators are met. Assets, data and decision rights must be turned over formally, with follow-up evaluation after closure. If continuation is unaffordable, partners should redesign the service early rather than hide the gap.

In conclusion, donor withdrawal becomes destructive when a project was never integrated into a lasting institution or budget. Local authority, affordable operating models and gradual transition can preserve useful services. A successful exit is not the disappearance of foreign logos; it is evidence that people can continue the benefit safely over the long term without depending on the original donor.

TASK 2 · 5

Why has public support for foreign aid weakened in some donor countries? How can aid agencies rebuild trust?

Optional collocation bank
information asymmetrybring onlinedevelopment dividendofficial aiddevelopment assistancehumanitarian aidbilateral aidmultilateral aidaid effectiveness
Model essay · 306 words

Public support for foreign aid has weakened in some donor countries because households face domestic pressure, scandals receive intense attention and agencies often explain success through remote statistics. Trust can be rebuilt only through honest evidence, visible accountability and a clearer account of why international cooperation serves both ethical duties and shared interests.

During housing, health or cost-of-living crises, voters may see every overseas payment as money withheld from local services. Political campaigns reinforce this false simplicity by presenting budgets as if one small aid cut would solve structural domestic shortages. At the same time, examples of corruption or expensive consultancy travel rapidly, while gradual improvements in child survival or tax administration are harder to show.

Aid communication can deepen the problem. Promotional stories sometimes portray recipients as passive and imply that one donation produced a complete transformation. When results later prove mixed, supporters feel misled. Complex reporting aimed at specialists also creates information asymmetry: citizens see a large expenditure total but cannot connect it to contracts, outcomes or failure. Repeatedly changing priorities makes long-term claims less credible.

Agencies should bring online clear project-level budgets, independent evaluations and explanations of uncertainty. They must report unsuccessful programmes and show what changed, while communities should describe results in their own voices. Procurement, overheads and complaints data need accessible context rather than unexplained spreadsheets. Governments should also explain how crisis prevention, public health and climate resilience create a shared development dividend, without pretending that aid replaces fair trade, debt reform or competent domestic policy.

In conclusion, trust will not return through more emotional advertising. It requires verifiable results, respect for the people represented and consistency between public promises and actual delivery. When agencies make scrutiny normal and show both limits and long-term value, citizens can judge aid as a serious public policy rather than an act of blind generosity.

Course index · Module 1 · Chapter 18

Academic English Studio · Topic 18 · Poverty, Foreign Aid and Sustainable Development


← Topic 17Course indexTopic 19 →
